title: Al-Andalus
subtitle: 1031–1492 · Taifas, Berber Dynasties and the Last Kingdom of Granada

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: From twenty-three taifas to the last sultanate of Granada

entries.label: The taifa kingdoms — fragmentation and patronage
entries.loc: Seville; Toledo; Zaragoza; Valencia; Granada (main taifas)
entries.hint: After the caliphate's collapse, al-Andalus fragments into 23 independent taifa kingdoms — each ruled by a former governor or military commander; the taifas pay tribute (parias) to the Christian kingdoms for protection; but they also become brilliant centres of poetry, music and philosophy.
entries.note: The taifa system: documented in Ibn Hayyan's Muqtabis (fragments) and Ibn Idhari, al-Bayan al-Mughrib (13th c., drawing on earlier sources). The parias payments: documented in Christian royal archives (Archivo de la Catedral de Toledo). Kennedy (1996), Muslim Spain and Portugal pp.130–160. The cultural flowering: best documented in the diwan (poetry collections) of taifa rulers, several of whom were themselves poets.

entries.label: Almoravid dynasty — Berber reform and reversal
entries.hint: The Almoravids arrive from Morocco at the taifas' invitation after Toledo falls (1085) — they defeat Alfonso VI at Sagrajas (1086), depose the taifa rulers, and temporarily reverse Christian advances; but their puritanical reform alienates the sophisticated Andalusian population.
entries.note: The Almoravid invitation: Ibn Idhari. The Battle of Sagrajas 23 Oct 1086: documented in both Arabic and Christian sources. The deposing of taifa rulers: documented in chronicles. Messier (2010), The Almoravids and the Meanings of Jihad: the standard modern study. The Almoravid architectural legacy in Marrakesh (UNESCO) and in Iberian buildings.

entries.label: Almohad Caliphate — the last North African empire in Iberia
entries.hint: The Almohads succeed the Almoravids — stricter Berber reformers who briefly exile Averroës (disgraced 1195, recalled to Marrakesh shortly before his death in 1198) and force Maimonides's family to flee Córdoba for Egypt; they build the Giralda tower in Seville (1184); their defeat at Las Navas (1212) begins the final collapse.
entries.note: The Giralda (1184): extant, Seville — the minaret built by the Almohads, now the cathedral's bell tower (UNESCO 1987). The exile of Averroës 1195: documented in Arabic sources. The defeat at Las Navas 1212: documented. Fromherz (2010), The Almohads: the standard modern study. The Almohad library programme: documented in their court records.

entries.label: Muhammad I founds the Nasrid Emirate of Granada
entries.hint: Muhammad I al-Ghalib founds the Nasrid dynasty in 1232 and begins the Alhambra fortress; he accepts tributary vassal status under Ferdinand III of Castile — a pragmatic bargain that keeps Granada independent for 250 more years; refugees from fallen Córdoba, Seville and Valencia enrich the new capital. His founding is not emergence from a vacuum but victory in a multi-way civil war: Ibn Hud of Murcia (r.1228-1238) briefly controls most of al-Andalus after the Almohad collapse — Seville, Cordoba and Granada all acknowledge him — before his assassination in 1238 opens the path for the Nasrids (entry k9, Third Taifa Period).
entries.note: Britannica: 'The first Naṣrid ruler, Muḥammad I al-Ghālib, a tributary vassal of the Christian king Ferdinand.' His accession 1238: documented in Arabic chronicles. The Alhambra's early construction: archaeology confirms 13th-c. foundations. Harvey (1992), Islamic Spain 1250–1500: the standard modern study. The influx of refugees from reconquered cities: documented in Arabic and Christian sources.

entries.label: Muhammad I to Muhammad IV — building the emirate (1273–1333)
entries.hint: Four sultans consolidate the Nasrid state after Muhammad I's foundational reign -- Muhammad II (1273-1302) navigates Marinid alliances and Castilian pressure; Muhammad III (1302-1309) briefly takes Ceuta; Nasr (1309-1314) and Ismail I (1314-1325) manage succession crises; Muhammad IV (1325-1333) is assassinated at 18; together they establish the Nasrid tributary system and begin systematic Alhambra construction.
entries.note: This entry covers a complex succession period. Individual reigns: Ibn al-Khatib, Ihata (primary) and al-Maqrizi for the Marinid-Nasrid relationship. Vidal Castro (1995), 'Historia politica', in Historia de Espana Menendez Pidal vol.VIII-3: the standard modern synthesis of early Nasrid political history.

entries.label: Yusuf I — builder of the Alhambra's towers and the madrasa
entries.hint: Yusuf I builds the Comares Tower and its magnificent throne room (1333-1348), the Puerta de la Justicia (1348) and funds the Madrasa Yusufiyya (1349) -- the first madrasa in al-Andalus -- alongside the maristán hospital; his reign is the high point of Nasrid architectural patronage and is cut short by assassination in 1354; Ibn al-Khatib (entry t4) serves in his court.
entries.note: Comares Tower construction date: inscription over the entrance (1334 AH 733/4) -- primary epigraphic evidence, extant. Puerta de la Justicia inscription 1348: extant. Madrasa Yusufiyya founding endowment: partial documentary evidence in Granadan notarial records. Yusuf I's assassination 1354: Ibn al-Khatib, Ihata -- primary chronicle.

entries.label: Muhammad V — the Alhambra at its peak
entries.hint: Muhammad V's two reigns see the completion of the Alhambra's greatest spaces — the Court of the Lions (1370s) and the Comares Hall; his court poet Ibn Zamrak inscribes poems on the walls of the palace; the Alhambra in its final form is his monument.
entries.note: Court of the Lions construction: dated by Ibn Zamrak's poems inscribed on the walls — the poem texts are the primary dating evidence. Ibn Zamrak's poetry on the Alhambra walls: primary source for the palace's meaning. Grabar (1978), The Alhambra: the standard art-historical study. Puerta Vílchez (2010), Reading the Alhambra: the most comprehensive study of the inscriptions.

entries.label: Muhammad XII (Boabdil) — the last sultan
entries.hint: Muhammad XII (Boabdil) surrenders Granada to Ferdinand and Isabella on 2 January 1492 — handing over the keys of the Alhambra; legend says he wept at the last view of his palace (the 'Moor's last sigh' pass); the fall of Granada completes the Reconquista.
entries.note: The surrender 2 Jan 1492: documented in both Spanish and Arabic sources. The capitulations (terms of surrender): Archivo General de Simancas — guaranteeing religious freedom for Muslims (later violated). Washington Irving's Tales of the Alhambra (1832): romanticised but influential account. Harvey (1992) pp.280–310. The keys of the Alhambra: traditional account.

entries.label: Ibn Hud and the Third Taifa Period -- the last civil war before Granada
entries.loc: Murcia (Ibn Hud's base); Seville; Cordoba; Granada
entries.hint: After the Almohad defeat at Las Navas (1212) the caliphate disintegrates into a Third Taifa period -- Ibn Hud al-Mutawakkil of Murcia (r.1228-1238) rises to briefly control most of al-Andalus, acknowledging the Abbasid Caliph of Baghdad rather than the Almohads; simultaneously Muhammad I al-Ghalib (the future Nasrid founder) builds his power base in Arjona and Jaen; Ibn Hud's assassination in Almeria in 1238 -- possibly arranged -- clears the path for the Nasrid consolidation; the Nasrid emirate is the survivor of this multi-way civil war, not an isolated foundation.
entries.note: Ibn Hud's proclamation as ruler of Murcia (1228) and rapid expansion: Ibn al-Khatib, Ihata -- primary chronicle. His acknowledgment of the Abbasid caliph (a rejection of Almohad legitimacy): documented in Arabic chronicle sources and numismatic evidence (coins struck in Baghdad caliph's name). His assassination in Almeria 1238: documented, circumstances disputed in sources. Kennedy (1996), Muslim Spain and Portugal: A Political History of al-Andalus: the standard English political narrative covering the Third Taifa period. The connection to k4 (Muhammad I): both are documented as rivals for control of the same cities in 1232-1238.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: The collapse of Muslim Spain — taifas, crusades and the final siege

entries.label: Almoravid conquest of al-Andalus (1086–1110)
entries.hint: The Almoravids cross from Morocco in response to taifa appeals after Toledo falls (1085, spain-718-1230) -- Yusuf ibn Tashfin defeats Alfonso VI at the Battle of Sagrajas (1086), then returns to depose the taifa kings one by one (1090-1102); Valencia falls after El Cid's death (1102); within 25 years the entire taifa mosaic is absorbed into a unified Almoravid al-Andalus.
entries.note: Battle of Sagrajas 23 Oct 1086: documented in Ibn al-Kardabus (Arabic chronicle) and Spanish Christian chronicles. The taifa annexations (1090-1102): Ibn al-Khatib and Ibn Idhari, al-Bayan al-Mughrib -- primary. Al-Mu'tamid of Seville's exile in Morocco after deposition 1091: documented in his own poetry (entry a2).

entries.label: Almohad conquest of al-Andalus (1147–1172)
entries.hint: The Almohads, having overthrown the Almoravids in Morocco, cross into Iberia in 1147 and systematically take the Almoravid-held cities -- Almeria (1147), Seville (1147), Badajoz (1151); they expel non-Muslims from certain cities (driving Maimonides's family from Cordoba, entry t2) and impose stricter religious conformity; Ibn Tufail (entry t6) and Averroes (entry t1) both serve the Almohad court despite the dynasty's theological conservatism.
entries.note: Almohad Iberian campaign 1147-1172: Ibn Sahib al-Sala, Al-Mann bil-Imama -- primary Almohad chronicle. The expulsion of non-Muslims from certain cities: documented in Jewish sources (Ibn Aqnin, commentary) and Christian chronicles. The apparent paradox of Averroes/Ibn Tufail serving the 'anti-philosophical' Almohads: discussed in Cruz Hernandez (1981) and Urvoy (1991).

entries.label: Battle of Alarcos (1195) -- the Almohads' greatest victory
entries.hint: Almohad Caliph Abu Yaqub Yusuf al-Mansur ('the Victorious') destroys the Castilian army of Alfonso VIII at Alarcos -- the most decisive Muslim military victory in Iberia since the 11th century; Alarcos castle falls, Alfonso barely escapes, and the Christian frontier is pushed back; the victory stabilises the Almohad empire for two more decades and directly motivates Pope Innocent III to call the crusading coalition that eventually defeats the Almohads at Las Navas de Tolosa (1212, entry c1) -- Alarcos is the immediate cause of Las Navas.
entries.note: Battle of Alarcos 18 Jul 1195: documented in both Arabic chronicles (Ibn al-Athir, al-Kamil) and Castilian sources (Lucas de Tuy, Cronica Mundi). Al-Mansur's victory titles (he takes the epithet al-Mansur 'the Victorious' after Alarcos): numismatic and epigraphy evidence. Pope Innocent III's crusade preaching in response: papal register, documented. Lomax (1978), The Reconquest of Spain: discusses Alarcos as the direct causal precedent for Las Navas. The 17-year gap between Alarcos (1195) and Las Navas (1212) filled by the papal coalition-building: documented in Spanish and French chronicle sources.

entries.label: Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa — already covered in spain-718-1230
entries.hint: The battle's significance is felt in this folio: after Las Navas, the Almohad empire collapses; within 40 years Córdoba (1236), Jaén (1246), Seville (1248) and Valencia (1238) fall; only Granada survives because it accepts Castilian vassalage.
entries.note: See spain-718-1230 folio for full documentation of the battle. The consequences here: Almohad collapse documented in Arabic sources. Ferdinand III's campaigns: documented in the Primera Crónica General (Alfonso X's chronicle, c.1270). The fall of Córdoba 29 Jun 1236: documented — the Great Mosque consecrated as a Christian church the same day. The fall of Seville 23 Nov 1248: documented.

entries.label: Granada as vassal — the 250-year survival strategy
entries.hint: The Nasrid Emirate survives for 250 years as a tributary vassal of Castile — paying annual tribute, providing military auxiliaries, and exploiting Christian internal conflicts; Muhammad I fought alongside Ferdinand III against Muslim opponents; survival through submission rather than resistance.
entries.note: Muhammad I's military service for Ferdinand III: documented in the Primera Crónica General. The annual tribute system: documented in Castilian royal accounts (AGS). Harvey (1992), Islamic Spain 1250–1500 ch.1. The exploitation of Christian civil wars (Pedro I vs Enrique II, 1366–1369): documented. Granada outlasts every other Muslim state in Iberia by adapting to vassal status.

entries.label: Mudéjar Revolt of 1264 — mass expulsion reshapes Andalusia
entries.loc: Seville; Jerez; Murcia (main centres of revolt)
entries.hint: Muslim populations (Mudéjars) in recently conquered Seville, Jerez and Murcia launch a major uprising backed by Muhammad I of Granada — the revolt is crushed by Castile with Aragonese help; the aftermath is the mass expulsion of Muslims from Castilian urban centres, transforming Andalusia's cities into purely Christian-settled areas and creating the hostile 'La Frontera' zone.
entries.note: The revolt: Crónica de Alfonso X (primary). Muhammad I of Granada's role: documented in both Castilian chronicles and Arabic sources. The expulsions: documented in subsequent repopulation charters (AGS). Burns (1973), Islam Under the Crusaders: the standard study of Mudéjar experience in the 13th century. The long-term demographic consequence: Andalusian cities lose their Muslim populations — documented in urban archaeology and subsequent census records. The lived experience of Mudejars (Muslims remaining under Christian rule after conquest) is documented in spain-1230-1492 entry lv6 -- this folio's c5 documents the revolt; the other folio documents the daily reality of those who did not revolt.

entries.label: Battle of Río Salado — the end of North African intervention
entries.hint: Alfonso XI of Castile and Alfonso IV of Portugal crush the combined armies of the Marinid Sultan of Morocco and the Nasrid Sultan of Granada at Río Salado — this catastrophic defeat ends large-scale imperial North African military intervention in the Iberian Peninsula -- small Marinid garrisons and ghuzat (volunteers of the faith) remain in Ronda and Málaga for decades, but no further major imperial invasion is attempted, leaving Granada completely isolated and entirely dependent on its defensive geography and tributary diplomacy.
entries.note: The battle 30 Oct 1340: documented in the Crónica de Alfonso XI (primary Castilian source). The Marinid forces: Ibn Khaldun, Muqaddima: contextualises the Marinid dynasty's involvement. The battle's long-term consequence: no further North African force crosses to Iberia after 1340 — documented through the absence of subsequent interventions. O'Callaghan (2011), The Gibraltar Crusade: the standard modern study. Lomax (1978), The Reconquista of Spain: contextualises Río Salado in the broader narrative. Cross-reference: spain-1230-1492 entry c8 (Battle of Rio Salado) documents this battle from the Castilian-Portuguese perspective.

entries.label: The fall of Granada — the last Reconquista campaign
entries.hint: Ferdinand and Isabella's systematic campaign against Granada (1481–1492) takes the remaining towns one by one — Alhama (1482), Ronda (1485), Málaga (1487), Baza (1489); Granada surrenders 2 January 1492 under capitulation terms that are violated within a decade.
entries.note: The capitulations 2 Jan 1492: AGS Patronato Real — primary text. The systematic campaign: documented in the Primera Crónica de Fernando e Isabel (published Pulgar 1490). Prescott (1838), History of the Reign of Ferdinand and Isabella: the classic account. Harvey (1992) pp.280–315. The violation of the capitulations (forced conversions begin c.1502): documented in royal decrees. Cross-reference: spain-1230-1492 entry c9 (Granada War) documents this campaign from the Castilian perspective; this entry is the same events seen from the Granadan side.

entries.label: Nasrid civil wars — faction warfare enables the final conquest
entries.hint: The final Castilian campaign exploits Granada's internal collapse — the rival noble clans (Abencerrajes and Zenetes) wage factional war; Muley Hacén is deposed by his son Boabdil (Muhammad XII); civil war then erupts between Boabdil and his uncle El Zagal (Muhammad XIII); Ferdinand and Isabella pick off isolated towns as the kingdom tears itself apart.
entries.note: The Nasrid dynastic conflict: documented in Arabic sources and in the Castilian court chronicles. The Abencerrajes and Zenetes: their rivalry documented in Arabic sources and in Washington Irving's (romanticised) Tales of the Alhambra. The deposition of Muley Hacén: documented. Boabdil's capture by Ferdinand at Lucena 1483: documented — he was released in exchange for a treaty recognising Castilian suzerainty. Harvey (1992), Islamic Spain pp.265–280: the standard account using Arabic and Castilian sources. Cross-reference: spain-1230-1492 entry c9 (Granada War) documents the Castilian campaign that exploits this internal collapse.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Nasrid administration and the capitulations of Granada

entries.label: The Nasrid diwan system -- secretaries, hajib and the divan al-insha
entries.hint: The Nasrid emirate sustains a sophisticated administrative apparatus for 250 years -- the hajib (chamberlain) acts as chief minister; the divan al-insha (chancery) produces official correspondence in an elaborate literary Arabic; the katibs (secretaries) are simultaneously officials and literary figures (Ibn al-Khatib, entry t4, being the supreme example); the Alhambra's administrative wing (Mexuar) documents the physical space of this bureaucracy; the chancery's letter-writing conventions become a model of Arabic prose.
entries.note: Nasrid chancery correspondence: partial survival in AHN Estado (Castilian archives received Granadan diplomatic letters, some survive). Ibn al-Khatib's Rayhanat al-kuttab: a treatise on chancery style -- primary. Arié (1973), L'Espagne musulmane au temps des Nasrides: the standard study of Nasrid administrative organisation. The Mexuar (administrative hall, Alhambra): extant, though much altered -- photographic and architectural survey documentation.

entries.label: Capitulations of Granada — the broken promise
entries.hint: The capitulations guaranteeing Muslims religious freedom, property rights and continued use of mosques are signed before the surrender — within a decade Archbishop Cisneros forces mass baptisms; the capitulations are formally the founding document of Spain's broken covenant with its Muslim population.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Capitulations text: AGS Patronato Real, leg.11, doc.27. Translated in Constable (1997), Medieval Iberia. The violation: Cisneros's forced conversions from 1499 — documented in his biographers and in AGS. Harvey (1990), Islamic Spain: the standard study of the Mudéjar and Morisco experience. The capitulations and their violation are the clearest documented case of Spain's treatment of its Muslim subjects.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: Almohad orthodoxy, Sufi mysticism and Ibn Arabi

entries.label: Maliki orthodoxy and the tension with rationalist philosophy
entries.hint: The dominant legal school of al-Andalus is Maliki fiqh -- orthodox, hadith-based, socially conservative and deeply suspicious of Greek-derived rationalist philosophy; the recurring persecution of philosophers (Ibn Bajja driven from Zaragoza, Averroes exiled by the Almohads in 1195, entry t1) reflects the Maliki ulama's political power to turn rulers against the rationalist tradition; the tension between legal orthodoxy and the philosophical tradition of Averroes and Ibn Tufail is the central intellectual drama of Andalusian thought.
entries.note: Maliki dominance in al-Andalus: documented from the 9th century; for this period, Ibn Rushd al-Jadd (Averroes's grandfather, a major Maliki jurist) represents the orthodox mainstream that his grandson's philosophy challenged. The 1195 Almohad condemnation of Averroes: documented in Ibn al-Abbar's biographical dictionary. Fierro (2005), 'The Legal Policies of the Almohads': the standard study of the Almohad ulama's power. The paradox: al-Andalus simultaneously produces Europe's most influential rational philosophy AND maintains one of its most rigorous orthodoxies.

entries.label: Almoravid Maliki rigidity and Almohad Tawhid -- two theological revolutions
entries.hint: The two Berber dynasties each import a theological revolution: the Almoravids impose a rigid Maliki legalism that leads to the public burning of al-Ghazali's Ihya Ulum al-Din (Revival of the Religious Sciences) in Cordoba in 1109 -- burning the most influential work of Islamic spirituality since the Quran; the Almohads go further, founding their movement on Ibn Tumart's doctrine of absolute divine unity (Tawhid) which rejects all four traditional legal schools as corrupted innovations and persecutes non-conformists; both revolutions alienate the sophisticated Andalusian population and neither survives contact with the peninsula's cultural complexity.
entries.note: The Cordoba burning of al-Ghazali's Ihya (1109, ordered by Almoravid authorities): documented in Ibn Khallikan's biographical dictionary and later Arabic sources -- one of the most documented acts of Islamic intellectual censorship. Ibn Tumart's Tawhid doctrine and the Almohad movement: Ibn Tumart's own Aqida (creed) survives -- primary text. Urvoy (1990), Penseurs d'al-Andalus: the standard study of the theological climate. The paradox that Averroes (entry t1) serves the anti-philosophical Almohads: discussed in Cruz Hernandez (1981) and directly connects this entry to t1's Almohad exile.

entries.label: Ibn Arabi — the greatest Sufi mystic of Islam
entries.hint: Muhyi al-Din ibn Arabi is born in Murcia, educated in Seville, and becomes the most influential mystic philosopher in Islamic history — his Fusus al-Hikam (Bezels of Wisdom) and Al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya (Meccan Revelations) synthesise Sufi thought; he is the 'Greatest Sheikh' (al-Shaykh al-Akbar).
entries.note: Al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya (Meccan Revelations): multiple manuscripts; Chittick (1989), The Sufi Path of Knowledge: the standard English study. Fusus al-Hikam: multiple manuscripts; Austin (1980), Bezels of Wisdom. Ibn Arabi's Andalusian origins: documented in his autobiography Ruh al-quds. Austin (1971), Sufis of Andalusia: documents his Andalusian teachers. Ibn Arabi spent his formative years in al-Andalus before leaving for Mecca in 1200.

entries.label: The Alhambra — a palace of Quranic inscriptions
entries.hint: The Alhambra's over 10,000 Arabic inscriptions are not merely decorative — the repeated Nasrid motto 'There is no victor but God' (la ghaliba illa Allah) is a theological-political claim: in a kingdom where the sultan is a vassal of Castile, divine sovereignty becomes the sole absolute sovereignty the court can assert; Quranic verses, Ibn Zamrak's poems and the motto together make the palace a theological argument in plaster and tile, distinct from its architectural register (entry a1) — belief literally inscribed into the built environment.
entries.note: Extant (UNESCO 1984). The inscriptions corpus: Puerta Vílchez (2010), Reading the Alhambra — the comprehensive catalogue of all inscriptions. The Ibn Zamrak poems (Muhammad V's court poet): primary source for the palace's self-understanding. The phrase 'Wa la ghalib illa Allah' (No victor but God): appears hundreds of times — the Nasrid motto. Grabar (1978), The Alhambra: the foundational art-historical analysis.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: The Alhambra, taifa poetry and the last flowering of Andalusian culture

entries.label: Wallada bint al-Mustakfi — the poet who refused the veil
entries.hint: Wallada, daughter of the last Umayyad caliph of Cordoba, holds literary salons after her father's death in 1025 -- embroidering her own verses on her robe ('I am fit for high positions / and am going my own way'); her passionate, erotic and satirical poems (some mocking her unfaithful lover Ibn Zaydun) are among the most distinctive in Arabic literature; she never married, refused the veil, and mixed freely with men in open violation of the conventions of the time -- a documented life of deliberate non-conformity.
entries.note: Wallada's poems: preserved in Ibn Bassam's Dhakhira (early 12th century anthology) and later biographical dictionaries -- the primary sources. Her embroidered garment verses: reported by multiple medieval Arabic biographers. The Ibn Zaydun correspondence (love poems from him, cutting satirical responses from her): documented in both poets' diwan collections, BNE Arabic manuscripts. Garulo (1986), La literatura arabe de al-Andalus durante el siglo XI: the standard modern study. She falls just before this folio's 1031 start but her documented literary activity runs through the taifa period.

entries.label: Ibn Hazm — Tawq al-Hamama (The Ring of the Dove)
entries.hint: Ibn Hazm's Tawq al-Hamama (c.1022) -- a treatise on love combining personal memoir, poetry and philosophical reflection -- is one of the most widely read Arabic prose texts in medieval and modern literature; equally important is Ibn Hazm as a Zahiri jurist and theologian whose al-Fasl fi al-Milal (Catalogue of Religions) is the first comparative religion study in Islamic literature; his Cordoban aristocratic background, political fall, and decades of literary production make him the defining intellectual of early taifa-period Iberia.
entries.note: Tawq al-Hamama (c.1022): multiple manuscript traditions, the Leiden manuscript being the oldest; Arberry's English translation (1953) is the standard accessible version. Al-Fasl fi al-Milal (completed c.1027-1030): BNE Arabic manuscript collection. Palacios (1927), Abenházam de Córdoba: the foundational Spanish study. Ibn Hazm's documented political trajectory (Umayyad loyalist, multiple imprisonments): cross-reference to the taifa political context of k1.

entries.label: Andalusian musical tradition — the nuba system
entries.hint: The Andalusian musical tradition (al-musiqa al-andalusiyya) develops in the taifa and Nasrid periods — the nuba (suite) system organises compositions by mode and rhythm; after 1492 the tradition is carried to Morocco, Algeria and Tunisia by exiles where it still survives as living performance practice.
entries.note: The nuba system: documented in Ibn Khaldun's Muqaddima (14th c.) — the most detailed medieval account. Farmer (1929), A History of Arabian Music: the standard older study. The survival in North Africa: documented in the contemporary practice of the Moroccan Andalusian orchestra. The connection to Ziryab's 9th-c. foundations: traced through Arabic musical theory treatises. Reynolds (2008), The Cambridge Companion to Medieval Music ch.12.

entries.label: Taifa courts as cultural patronage states
entries.hint: The taifa rulers compensate for political weakness with cultural patronage — al-Mu'tamid of Seville is himself a celebrated poet, al-Muqtadir of Zaragoza builds the Aljafería palace (extant, UNESCO), the Banu Hud patronise music and science; the taifa courts are the peak of Andalusian cultural production.
entries.note: Al-Mu'tamid's poetry: diwan preserved in multiple manuscripts; Nykl (1946), Hispano-Arabic Poetry. The Aljafería palace (c.1065): extant, Zaragoza (UNESCO). Al-Muqtadir's court: documented in Ibn Idhari. Scales (1994), The Fall of the Caliphate, ch.4–5. The Aljafería is the best-preserved taifa palace — its archaeology provides the primary material evidence for taifa court culture.

entries.label: Al-Mu'tamid of Seville — the poet-king
entries.hint: Al-Mu'tamid ibn Abbad, ruler of Seville (1069–1091), is the greatest poet of the taifa era — he writes with his court poet Ibn Ammar, rules a brilliant court, and ends his life in exile in Morocco after inviting the Almoravids who then depose him; his elegy from exile is one of the most moving Arabic poems.
entries.note: Al-Mu'tamid's diwan: manuscripts preserved in multiple Arabic libraries; Nykl (1946), Hispano-Arabic Poetry: selections translated. His elegy from Moroccan exile: widely anthologised. The story of his relationship with Ibn Ammar: documented in biographical sources. He died in Aghmat, Morocco, 1095 — his tomb became a pilgrimage site. García Gómez (1944), Poemas arabigoandaluces: the standard Spanish anthology.

entries.label: Muwashshah and zajal -- the Hispano-Arabic poetic synthesis
entries.hint: The muwashshah (strophic poem in classical Arabic with a Romance-language refrain, the kharja) and the zajal (strophic poem in colloquial Arabic) are al-Andalus's most original literary contribution -- the kharjas are the earliest documented Romance vernacular poetry in history, predating the Provençal troubadours; Ibn Quzmán of Cordoba (c.1078-1160) is the master of the zajal form, his 149 surviving zajals the primary corpus; the influence on European troubadour lyric (via the Provençal courts) is debated but the structural parallels are documented; the forms survive in North African music after 1492.
entries.note: The kharjas (Romance-language refrains): discovered by Samuel Stern in 1948 in Cairo Geniza manuscripts -- a 20th-century scholarly discovery of 11th-century poems. Ibn Quzmán's Diwan (149 zajals): Arabic manuscript tradition, modern critical edition by Corriente (1980) and Garcia Gomez (1972). Monroe (1974), Hispano-Arabic Poetry: A Student Anthology: the standard English collection with scholarly apparatus. The troubadour connection (Menocal 1987, The Arabic Role in Medieval Literary History, vs Spanke's skepticism): the debate is documented and ongoing -- noted in the entry rather than asserting a single view.

entries.label: The Alhambra — the palace of water, light and inscription
entries.hint: The Alhambra is the most complete surviving medieval Islamic palace complex in the world — the Comares Hall (throne room), Court of the Lions (begun 1370s), Generalife gardens; its system of water channels, stucco decoration and calligraphic inscriptions are the primary evidence for Nasrid court culture.
entries.note: Extant (UNESCO 1984). Ongoing ALHAMAT archaeological project (Cambridge 2021–2022): documents building phases using radiocarbon dating — published Antiquity 2025. Grabar (1978), The Alhambra: the foundational study. The Court of the Lions: dated by Ibn Zamrak's inscribed poems. Dodds (1992), Architecture and Ideology in Early Medieval Spain: contextualises within Iberian tradition.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Averroës, Maimonides and the philosophical Golden Age

entries.label: Ibn Hazm's Zahiri jurisprudence -- against legal consensus
entries.hint: Ibn Hazm's adoption and development of Zahiri (literalist) legal methodology -- rejecting the consensus-based reasoning (qiyas, ijma) of the dominant Maliki school in favour of pure textual interpretation -- makes him the most intellectually radical jurist of medieval Islam and earns him the enmity of the Maliki ulama who burn his books; his al-Muhalla (11 volumes) is the comprehensive Zahiri legal code; his critique of Maliki consensus directly anticipates modern Islamic reformism and was explicitly cited by 20th-century salafi thinkers.
entries.note: Al-Muhalla bi al-Athar: printed edition Cairo 1347-1352 AH; manuscript tradition traced to 12th century. The book-burnings by Maliki ulama: documented in Ibn Hayyan and later biographical sources. Goldziher (1884), 'The Zahiris': the foundational Western study. Arnaldez (1956), Grammaire et theologie chez Ibn Hazm de Cordoue: the standard French study. His 20th-century citation by Salafi reformers (explicitly by Bin Baz): documented -- a remarkable 900-year intellectual afterlife.

entries.label: Ibn Bajja (Avempace) — the regime of the solitary
entries.loc: Zaragoza (born; court of Banu Hud); Seville; Fez (died)
entries.hint: Ibn Bajja (Avempace) argues in Tadbir al-Mutawahhid (The Regime of the Solitary) that the rational thinker must intellectually isolate from a corrupt society to achieve union with the Active Intellect — the foundational text for both Averroës and Maimonides; his political philosophy directly shapes the tradition of Andalusi rationalism.
entries.note: Tadbir al-Mutawahhid: Arabic manuscript — partial critical edition Asín Palacios (1946), El régimen del solitario. The Active Intellect theory: Ibn Bajja's adaptation of al-Farabi documented in the text. Genequand (2001), 'Ibn Bajja et la science divine' in Mélanges de l'Université Saint-Joseph: the standard modern study. The connection to Averroës: explicitly acknowledged in Ibn Rushd's own commentary on De Anima. The connection to Maimonides: documented in Guide for the Perplexed I.74 — he cites Ibn Bajja.

entries.label: Averroës (Ibn Rushd) — Aristotle reaches Europe through Córdoba
entries.hint: Ibn Rushd (Averroës) writes commentaries on virtually all of Aristotle's works between 1169 and 1195 — translated into Latin and Hebrew, they make Aristotle accessible to medieval Europe for the first time; his medical encyclopaedia Colliget becomes a European textbook; he is exiled by the Almohads in 1195.
entries.note: Averroës's commentaries (1169–1195): Arabic originals and Latin translations. Major Latin manuscripts: BnF and British Library. Colliget (Al-Kulliyat fi al-Tibb): translated as Colliget — European university textbook for centuries. The exile of 1195: documented in Arabic sources. Taylor & López-Farjeat (2016), The Routledge Companion to Islamic Philosophy: the standard survey. The 'Commentator' sobriquet (Dante calls him this): documented in medieval European sources.

entries.label: Maimonides (Ibn Maymun) — Guide for the Perplexed
entries.hint: Moses ben Maimon (Maimonides) is born in Córdoba in 1138 — forced to flee the Almohad persecution, he eventually settles in Egypt; his Guide for the Perplexed (c.1190) reconciles Aristotelian philosophy with Jewish theology and becomes the most influential medieval Jewish philosophical text.
entries.note: Guide for the Perplexed (Dalalat al-Ha'irin, c.1190): Arabic original; Hebrew translation by Samuel ibn Tibbon (1204) — BnF. Maimonides's birth in Córdoba 1138: documented. The Almohad persecution forcing his family to flee: documented in his own writings. Pines (1963), Guide of the Perplexed (Chicago): the standard English translation. His Mishneh Torah (Hebrew law code, 1180): the other major work.

entries.label: Ibn Tufail — Hayy ibn Yaqzan, the first philosophical novel
entries.hint: Ibn Tufail's Hayy ibn Yaqzan (The Living, Son of the Awake, c.1165) is the first philosophical novel in history — a feral child on a desert island discovers through pure reason and observation alone the supreme truths of nature, metaphysics and God; it directly influences John Locke's tabula rasa and serves as a major precursor to the European Enlightenment.
entries.note: Hayy ibn Yaqzan (c.1165): Arabic manuscripts; first Latin translation Pococke (1671) as Philosophus Autodidactus — the vehicle of its European influence. English translation Goodman (2009), Ibn Tufayl's Hayy Ibn Yaqzan. The influence on Locke: documented in Locke's library catalogue (Christ Church Oxford) — he owned a copy of the 1671 Latin translation. Russell (1994), 'The Impact of the Philosophus Autodidactus': the standard study of its European impact. Ibn Tufail's role at the Almohad court: documented in biographic dictionaries.

entries.label: Ibn Sab'in -- absolute monism and the Sicilian Questions
entries.hint: Ibn Sab'in of Murcia develops a school of absolute monism (Wahdat al-Wujud in its most radical form, distinct from Ibn Arabi's version) and around 1240 receives a set of philosophical questions from Holy Roman Emperor Frederick II -- the Sicilian Questions, on the eternity of the world, the nature of the soul and the categories of Aristotle; his written responses constitute a documented direct intellectual exchange between a Muslim philosopher and a Christian emperor, an extraordinary cross-confessional philosophical dialogue; he dies by ritual self-inflicted wound in Mecca in 1271, one of the most enigmatic deaths in medieval intellectual history.
entries.note: The Sicilian Questions: Ibn Sab'in's responses survive in manuscript (Arabic) -- edited by Massignon (1931), Rasail Ibn Sab'in. Frederick II's documented interest in Islamic philosophy (his court in Palermo as an intellectual crossroads): cross-reference with the Almohad-era Andalusian philosophical tradition. Akasoy (2006), 'Ibn Sab'in's Sicilian Questions', Recherches de Theologie: the standard modern study. The self-inflicted death in Mecca: reported in multiple Arabic biographical sources though some dispute the details. His radical monism's relationship to Ibn Arabi (entry r1): both use Wahdat al-Wujud but Ibn Sab'in pushes it to a more absolute position.

entries.label: Ibn al-Khatib — vizier, historian and voice of the Alhambra
entries.loc: Granada (court of Yusuf I and Muhammad V); Fez (exile and death)
entries.hint: Lisan al-Din ibn al-Khatib is the dominant intellectual figure of Nasrid Granada — chief minister (vizier) under Yusuf I and Muhammad V, author of the Ihata (the definitive history of Granada), and poet whose verses are inscribed on the walls of the Alhambra's Court of the Myrtles; his dramatic fall from favour, exile and execution in Fez embodies late Nasrid political volatility.
entries.note: The Ihata fi akhbar Gharnata (History of Granada): multiple Arabic manuscripts — the definitive history of the Nasrid kingdom. Ibn al-Khatib's poems in the Alhambra: identified and documented in Puerta Vílchez (2010), Reading the Alhambra — his verses appear in the Court of the Myrtles. His execution in Fez 1374: documented in Arabic sources — strangled in prison on charges of heresy brought by enemies in Granada. Harvey (1992), Islamic Spain 1250–1500 ch.8: the standard English account.

entries.label: Ibn Khaldun — the Muqaddima and the philosophy of history
entries.hint: Ibn Khaldun visits Granada briefly but his Muqaddima (Prolegomena, 1377) — the introduction to his universal history — is deeply shaped by his Andalusian cultural inheritance; it is the first work to propose a cyclical theory of history based on social cohesion (asabiyya) and is sometimes called the first work of sociology.
entries.note: Muqaddima (1377): Arabic manuscripts in multiple libraries; Rosenthal translation (Princeton 1967), 3 vols. Ibn Khaldun's visit to Granada and meeting with Pedro I of Castile: documented in his Autobiography (Ta'rif). Lacoste (1984), Ibn Khaldun: the Birth of History and the Past of the Third World: the standard modern study. His Andalusian connections: documented in the autobiography.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Trade, silk and the economy of the last Islamic state

entries.label: The parias system — tribute funding Christian expansion
entries.hint: The taifa kings pay annual tribute (parias) to the Christian kingdoms in exchange for military protection and non-aggression -- a fiscal transfer that simultaneously funds Christian castle-building, mercenary armies and eventually the Reconquista; the payments are documented in substantial sums (Toledo's taifa paid enormous annual tribute to Alfonso VI); the system creates a fiscal dependency that enriches the Christian north while draining the taifa south, and directly motivates the Almoravid intervention when the taifas can no longer pay.
entries.note: Parias payments: documented in Christian charter sources (Garcia Gallo, Homenaje: reconstructs payment amounts from Leonese and Castilian royal archives). Wasserstein (1985), The Rise and Fall of the Party-Kings: the standard English study quantifying parias as a structural mechanism of taifa fragility. The connection to Almoravid intervention (entry c7): the taifas' inability to sustain both parias AND internal patronage is the fiscal logic of inviting the Almoravids.

entries.label: Almeria and Malaga -- al-Andalus's commercial ports
entries.hint: Almeria is the wealthiest city of taifa-era al-Andalus -- its silk weaving and trans-Mediterranean trade (with Egypt, the Levant, and North Africa) make it a commercial powerhouse; a 10th-century geographer counted 800 textile workshops; Malaga succeeds it as the primary Nasrid commercial port, connecting Granada's silk economy (entry s1) to the wider Mediterranean; both cities' customs revenues are a primary source of Nasrid state income and are the commercial foundation for the emirate's 250-year survival.
entries.note: Almeria's 800 workshops: al-Razi (10th century) cited in later geographers -- primary, though the figure may be rhetorical. Malaga's role in Nasrid trade: documented in Ibn Khaldun's Muqaddima and Nasrid fiscal records partially preserved in AHN. Lopez (1952), 'The Trade of Medieval Europe: The South', Cambridge Economic History of Europe vol.II: the standard overview placing al-Andalus within Mediterranean trade networks.

entries.label: Granada's silk economy — the maristán and the silk market
entries.hint: The Nasrid Emirate of Granada sustains its independence partly through a sophisticated silk economy — the Vega de Granada produces raw silk exported across the Mediterranean; the Alcaicería (silk market) and the maristán (hospital funded by silk revenues) document a complex urban economy.
entries.note: The Alcaicería: documented in Arabic sources and urban archaeology. The maristán (hospital, c.1367): foundation inscription survives — it records that Muhammad V funded it from silk revenues (Puerta Vílchez 2010). Constable (1994), Trade and Traders in Muslim Spain: the standard economic study. The silk export documentation: Genoese merchant records (Archivio di Stato, Genoa) document Granadan silk exports.

entries.label: The Genoese-Granada alliance -- silk, sugar and the fondaco network
entries.hint: Genoese merchants hold a virtual monopoly on the maritime export of Granadan silk, dried fruit and sugar to northern Europe -- operating permanent fondacos (trading warehouses with residential quarters) in Malaga and Almeria, negotiating formal commercial treaties with Nasrid sultans from the late 13th century; the commercial alliance is the financial mechanism behind Granada's 250-year survival: Genoese credit, ships and commercial networks convert the Vega's silk production (entry s1) into the revenues that fund both the Nasrid court and the parias payments to Castile; notably, the same Genoese commercial network that finances Granadan silk also eventually finances Columbus's Castilian voyage.
entries.note: Genoese-Granadan commercial treaties (from c.1278 under Muhammad II): Archivio di Stato di Genova, diplomatic series -- primary. The fondacos in Malaga and Almeria: documented in Genoese notarial records and partially in AHN. Lopez (1943), 'Majorcans and Genoese on the North African Route in the Late Thirteenth Century', Revue belge de philologie et d'histoire: the standard study of the Genoese commercial network in the western Mediterranean. The Columbus connection: Columbus was Genoese and the same commercial networks financing Granadan silk are documented as providing contacts and potentially partial financing for the Atlantic venture (cross-reference spain-1230-1492, world entry w1).

entries.label: Black Death in al-Andalus — Ibn Khatima's treatise
entries.hint: The Black Death reaches al-Andalus via Almería in 1348 — the Almerian physician Ibn Khatima writes the first Arabic treatise specifically on plague, proposing a theory of contagion through tiny bodies entering the body; his Andalusian colleague Ibn al-Khatib writes a second treatise independently reaching similar conclusions.
entries.note: Ibn Khatima, Tahqiq al-naba' an amr al-waba' (1349): Arabic manuscript — Biblioteca Nacional de España. Ibn al-Khatib, Muqni'at al-sa'il (c.1362): also Arabic manuscript. Both propose germ-like theories of contagion 500 years before Pasteur. Dols (1977), The Black Death in the Middle East: the standard study. The Tàrrega pogrom 1348 (see Lives): documented genetically (MDPI 2026 study).

entries.label: The astrolabe and al-Zarqali's astronomical instruments
entries.hint: Ibrahim ibn Yahya al-Zarqali (Arzachel), working in Toledo before its fall, refines the astrolabe into the universal 'saphea' (capable of use at any latitude) and compiles the Toledo Tables (1080) -- astronomical tables that become the most accurate in the medieval world; translated into Latin as the Toledan Tables, they are used by European astronomers for over a century and directly inform Alfonso X's Alfonsine Tables (cross-reference spain-1230-1492, entry t1); al-Zarqali also determines the precession of the equinoxes more accurately than Ptolemy.
entries.note: Al-Zarqali's Toledo Tables (1080): Latin manuscript tradition (Toledan Tables) documented in medieval library catalogues across Europe. The saphea astrolabe: extant examples in the Museo Arqueologico Nacional, Madrid and Museo de la Alhambra. Millás Vallicrosa (1943), Estudios sobre Azarquiel: the standard study. The direct lineage to Alfonso X's Alfonsine Tables (cross-reference spain-1230-1492, t1): explicitly acknowledged in the Alfonsine Tables' own preface, which cites al-Zarqali as the primary predecessor.

entries.label: Andalusi hydraulic engineering -- acequias, norias and the Alhambra's water
entries.hint: Al-Andalus inherits and perfects Roman and Iranian hydraulic techniques -- the acequia (irrigation channel) systems of the Vega de Granada, the Valencia huerta and the Murcia vegas are documented engineering networks using gravity-fed channels, distribution weirs and the noria (waterwheel) to sustain intensive agriculture in a semi-arid landscape; the Alhambra's water system is the apex: the Acequia Real brings water from the Darro river to supply the palace gardens, fountains and hammams through a network of pipes and channels that defies the hilltop topography; Mudéjar farmers maintain this system after 1492 (cross-reference spain-1230-1492, entry lv6).
entries.note: The Acequia Real del Generalife: documented in Nasrid endowment records (waqf) and modern archaeological survey (Malpica Cuello, 1995, El sistema de irrigacion del generalife). Valencia acequia system: documented in the Tribunal de las Aguas records (cross-reference spain-1230-1492, entry lv6 note). Glick (1970), Irrigation and Society in Medieval Valencia: the standard English study. Watson (1983), Agricultural Innovation in the Early Islamic World: contextualises the acequia system within the broader Islamic agricultural revolution.

entries.label: Paper manufacture -- Xativa and the book culture of al-Andalus
entries.hint: Xativa (Shatibi) is al-Andalus's primary paper-producing centre -- paper mills using techniques from the Islamic east produce the material that enables al-Andalus's extraordinary book culture (documented private libraries of thousands of volumes, public libraries in Cordoba); Andalusian paper and binding techniques pass to Christian Spain after Xativa's conquest (1244, cross-reference spain-1230-1492, entry te2) and transform European manuscript production; the book as an object of knowledge circulation is an Andalusian-mediated technology.
entries.note: Xativa paper production: documented in Arabic geographical sources (al-Idrisi, Kitab Rujar, 1154 -- primary) and continued after Christian conquest (cross-reference spain-1230-1492, te2 note). The private libraries of Cordoba (the caliph's library reportedly 400,000 volumes): rhetorical figure but attesting to real book culture -- documented in al-Maqqari. Lévi-Provençal (1950), Histoire de l'Espagne musulmane vol.3, ch.XIV: the standard study of Andalusian book culture and paper's role.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: Toledo translations, diplomatic exchange and the Andalusian diaspora

entries.label: The Andalusian diaspora — knowledge carries west and east
entries.hint: Each Christian conquest sends waves of Andalusian scholars and artisans into the Islamic world — after 1492, the entire surviving Muslim community of Spain (Mudejars) is expelled or forced to convert; they carry Andalusian architecture, music, cuisine and scholarship to Morocco and the Ottoman Empire.
entries.note: The Nasrid refugees after 1492: Harvey (1990), Islamic Spain — the standard study. The Andalusian architectural influence in Morocco: the Bou Inania madrasa in Fez (1351) uses craftsmen from Granada — documented. The survival of Andalusian music in North Africa: Reynolds (2008). The genetic evidence for the diaspora: recent DNA studies document Sephardic and Morisco genetic clusters in Morocco and Turkey.

entries.label: Andalusian scholarship transmitted to Europe via Toledo
entries.hint: The conquest of Toledo (1085, spain-718-1230 folio) opens the city's libraries to European scholars -- the Andalusian philosophical and scientific corpus (Averroes's Aristotle commentaries, al-Zarqali's astronomical tables, Ibn Sina's medical encyclopaedia) passes into Latin translation and from there into European university curricula; this folio's entry covers the OUTPUT from al-Andalus's intellectual tradition; the translation school as a Christian institution is documented in spain-1230-1492 (entry t4).
entries.note: Michael Scot's translations of Averroës (c.1217 onwards): Latin manuscripts in BnF and British Library — he translated the major commentaries on Aristotle while working in Toledo and Sicily. Herman the German (Hermannus Alemannus, mid-13th c.): further translations. Gerard of Cremona (d.1187) did not translate Averroës — chronologically impossible as Averroës's major commentaries (1169–1195) postdate Cremona's death. Burnett (2001), 'The Coherence of the Arabic-Latin Translation Program in Toledo'. The impact on European universities: Averroës becomes required reading at Paris by the 1240s.

entries.label: The Strait of Gibraltar -- contested crossing between worlds
entries.hint: The Strait of Gibraltar is the most heavily contested maritime choke-point in the medieval West -- controlling it determines whether Berber dynastic reinforcements can reach al-Andalus; the Almoravids and Almohads both cross it to intervene; the Marinids fight Castile, Aragon and Granada over it for decades (1275-1340); its closure to North African forces after Rio Salado (1340, entry c4) seals Granada's isolation; the Strait is simultaneously a commercial lane -- Genoese and Catalan ships pass through it linking the Mediterranean to the Atlantic, making Malaga and Almeria key nodes in the inter-sea trade.
entries.note: The 'Battle of the Strait' concept: O'Callaghan (2011), The Gibraltar Crusade: the standard modern study using Castilian and Moroccan sources. The Marinid crossings: documented in both Arabic and Castilian chronicles. The strategic importance of Tarifa: its capture by Castile (1292) and the defence by Guzmán el Bueno (documented) — a turning point. The 1340 Río Salado and 1344 capture of Algeciras: Crónica de Alfonso XI (primary). The Strait's closure: the geopolitical endpoint of North African intervention in Europe.

entries.label: Al-Andalus and the African world -- gold, slaves and the Catalan Atlas
entries.loc: Seville; Almeria; Mali (Mansa Musa depicted in the Catalan Atlas)
entries.hint: Andalusian merchants and geographers maintain documentary connections to sub-Saharan Africa through the Saharan gold trade -- the Catalan Atlas (1375, cross-reference spain-1230-1492 te1) shows Mansa Musa of Mali holding a gold nugget, drawn from information circulating in the Andalusian-North African commercial network; al-Idrisi's geography (1154) describes West African kingdoms in detail; the gold of Mali that funds Granada's silk economy and the parias payments to Castile partly originates in this trans-Saharan network; enslaved Africans documented in Granada's notarial records (cross-reference lv new entry) are part of the same network.
entries.note: Al-Idrisi, Kitab Rujar (1154): primary geographical source on sub-Saharan Africa, written for Roger II of Sicily but drawing on Andalusian geographical tradition. The Catalan Atlas (1375): cross-reference spain-1230-1492 te1. Andalusian merchants in North African trans-Saharan trade: documented in Cairo Geniza records (Goitein, 1967-1988, A Mediterranean Society). Constable (1994), Trade and Traders in Muslim Spain: the standard study of Andalusian commercial networks including the African dimension.

entries.label: Treaties of Cazola (1179) and Almizra (1244) -- al-Andalus pre-divided
entries.hint: Before al-Andalus is conquered, the Christian kingdoms divide it between themselves -- the Treaty of Cazola (1179) assigns Murcia and the southeast to Castile; the Treaty of Almizra (1244, signed as James I completes the Valencia conquest) finalises the division; from Granada's perspective these treaties are an encirclement agreement, fixing the territorial ceiling within which the Nasrid emirate will be permanently confined; the treaties explain why Granada never tries to recover Murcia or Valencia -- those territories have been assigned to a specific Christian partner whose protection the Nasrids partly depend on.
entries.note: Treaty of Cazola 1179: AHN, and in documentary collections of Alfonso VIII (Gonzalez, Alfonso VIII, vol.2). Treaty of Almizra 20 Mar 1244: Archivo de la Corona de Aragon -- the original survives. Gonzalez Jimenez (2004), Alfonso X el Sabio: discusses the implementation of the Cazola partition in the context of the Mudejar revolt of 1264 (entry c5). The Nasrid perspective on encirclement: implicit in the tributary diplomacy documented in w2 -- the sultans navigate within boundaries fixed by treaties they had no part in making.

entries.label: Nasrid diplomacy -- survival through navigation between powers
entries.hint: The Nasrid emirate's 250-year survival is fundamentally a diplomatic achievement -- Muhammad I's acceptance of Castilian vassalage (1246) establishes the template; subsequent sultans balance between Marinid Morocco (military ally but also threatening), Castile (overlord but also protector against Aragon), and from the 1450s the Ottoman Empire (potential patron); Nasrid envoys reach Cairo, the Hafsid court in Tunis, and after 1453 Constantinople; Granada's ambassadors are documented in European and Islamic sources as highly literate representatives of a sophisticated chancery tradition (entry o3).
entries.note: Muhammad I's 1246 vassalage agreement with Ferdinand III: cross-reference spain-1230-1492 entry k1 (Ferdinand III) and spain-1230-1492 entry c2 (James I of Aragon). Nasrid-Marinid diplomatic correspondence: documented in Ibn Khaldun (entry t3) and in the AHN Estado collection of Arabic diplomatic documents. Nasrid-Ottoman contacts (post-1453): documented in Arié (1973), ch.VIII. Harvey (1990), Islamic Spain 1250-1500: the standard English study of Nasrid diplomacy.

entries.label: 1492 — three events in one year
entries.hint: 1492 is the hinge year of Spanish history: Granada falls (2 January), the Jews are expelled (31 March Alhambra Decree), and Columbus departs for the Americas (3 August) — all three authorised by the same monarchs from the same palace; the end of medieval Spain and the beginning of the global empire.
entries.note: The Alhambra Decree (31 Mar 1492): AGS Patronato Real — expelling all unbaptised Jews. Columbus's departure: documented in his journal (copy: BnF). The co-incidence of these three events in one year from the Alhambra: the central argument of Colon (2004), 1492: The Poetics of Diaspora. The three events' connection: explicitly noted by contemporaries including Peter Martyr d'Anghiera in letters dated that year.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored · including categorical shifts

entries.label: The Saqaliba -- Slavic slaves who became taifa kings
entries.hint: After the collapse of the Cordoba caliphate (1031), several former royal palace slaves of Slavic origin (saqaliba -- 'Slavs', applied broadly to northern European slaves) seize power in the eastern taifas: Khayran al-Amiri rules Almeria, Mujahid al-Amiri rules Denia (and briefly Sardinia), Labib rules Tortosa; these men, castrated and enslaved in youth, educated in the caliphal palace and trained as military commanders, rise to found dynasties -- the most dramatic documented case of enslaved-to-ruler social mobility in medieval Europe.
entries.note: Khayran al-Amiri of Almeria (r.1012-1028): documented in Ibn Hayyan's Muqtabis -- primary chronicle. Mujahid al-Amiri of Denia (r.1010-1044) and his Sardinian campaigns: documented in Ibn al-Khatib and Italian chronicle sources (a cross-Mediterranean primary source convergence). Wasserstein (1985), The Rise and Fall of the Party-Kings, ch.5: the standard modern study of Saqaliba taifa rulers. Pelteret (1995), Slavery in Early Mediaeval England: comparative context for the Slavic slave trade that supplied Andalusian markets.

entries.label: Samuel ibn Naghrela (HaNagid) -- Jewish vizier and Hebrew poet
entries.hint: Samuel ibn Naghrela, known as HaNagid ('the Prince'), serves as vizier (chief minister) of the Zirid taifa of Granada from 1027 while simultaneously being the nagid (head) of the Jewish community of al-Andalus -- he commands armies, negotiates treaties, and writes Hebrew poetry about his campaigns; his son Joseph inherits the vizierate but is killed in the Granada pogrom of December 1066 (the Muslim population kills the Jewish community, estimated at thousands) -- one of the first mass killings of Jews in al-Andalus, prefiguring the 1391 massacres in Christian Spain (cross-reference spain-1230-1492 lv4).
entries.note: Samuel HaNagid's diwan (Hebrew war poetry): published in modern critical edition (Schirmann). His vizierate: documented in Arabic sources (Ibn al-Khatib, Ihata) and confirmed by Hebrew sources -- a rare case of full cross-linguistic primary documentation. The 1066 Granada pogrom: Ibn Hazm's contemporary Arabic account and Hebrew lament poetry (documented in Lewis, 1984, The Jews of Islam). Cole (2007), The Dream of the Poem: Hebrew Poetry from Muslim and Christian Spain: the standard English anthology and study.

entries.label: The 'lady of the Alhambra' — Nasrid women in inscriptions
entries.hint: The Alhambra's inscriptions include dedications to royal women — the Mexuar hall carries inscriptions referencing the sultan's mother; Nasrid women are documented as patrons of religious foundations; the Generalife gardens are associated with female royal use; the evidence is fragmented but present.
entries.note: Alhambra inscriptions mentioning royal women: Puerta Vílchez (2010) catalogues them. The patronage of religious foundations by Nasrid women: Harvey (1992). The Generalife: its association with female royal quarters documented in Arabic literary sources. The Moroccan connection — Nasrid women's letters preserved in Moroccan archives (unpublished): noted in Harvey (1992) as an under-researched area.

entries.label: Enslaved Africans in Nasrid Granada -- the notarial record
entries.hint: Nasrid Granada's notarial records (preserved in the AHN and Archivo de la Alhambra) document a significant enslaved African population -- individual manumission documents, sale records and waqf endowments reference enslaved people by name, origin (described as 'black' or sub-Saharan African) and sometimes occupation; the Granada silk economy (entry s1) and the trans-Saharan gold-and-slave network (entry w3) together produce a documented Afro-Andalusian presence that the Christian conquest of 1492 transforms, sending some enslaved people to Christian ownership while others are freed by the capitulations -- a lived experience at the intersection of the folio's economic and political stories.
entries.note: Nasrid notarial records including manumission and sale documents: AHN Clero and Archivo de la Alhambra, partially published in Lopez Beltrán (1986) and Seco de Lucena Paredes (1961). Martínez Enamorado (2003), 'Esclavos negros en el reino nazarí': the standard modern study of the Granadan African enslaved population. The connection to the trans-Saharan network (w3): documented commercial links in Constable (1994). The 1492 capitulations' treatment of enslaved people: Ladero Quesada (1993), Granada.

events.l: Battle of Sagrajas -- Almoravids defeat Alfonso VI
Battle of Alarcos -- Almohads crush Castile
Mudéjar Revolt — mass expulsion of Muslims
Battle of Río Salado — North Africa halted
Ibn Arabi born — greatest Sufi mystic
Wallada -- the poet who refused the veil
Ibn Hazm: Ring of the Dove
Ibn al-Khatib — vizier and historian of Granada
Parias system -- tribute funding Christian expansion
Al-Zarqali -- Toledo Tables and the astrolabe
Acequias, norias and the Alhambra water system
Battle of the Strait — Gibraltar contested
Saqaliba -- enslaved Slavs who became taifa kings
Samuel HaNagid -- Jewish vizier and the 1066 pogrom
